I've played since a little after launch, so the game's always been special to me. It's fun, has crisp gameplay, and has a great cast of characters. The game feels so stable and solid, it's relatively fair and balanced compared to other similar games, and there's so much flexibility in how you can play. It's just entirely unique and undefeated in its genre. I do love Marvel Rivals a lot, almost equally, but this game is just something else when it comes to the shooter feel. However, I won't ever forgive Blizzard for the ♥♥♥♥♥♥ broken promises and betrayal of their playerbase. I've had long stretches of breaks too, and felt incredibly sour about the state of the game in the past. But the game is still fun and gets consistent updates. And right now is a great time to hop in after the five new characters, story updates, and the future plans the team has revealed to us. Not to mention the player count has skyrocketed. The devs genuinely seem passionate about this game, and I really feel it's been evil, greedy leadership that led to their terrible decisions in the past. I hope they keep this momentum, and maybe it'll recover from the review-bombing (doubtful though lol).

There is a strange kind of love that feels like suffering and that is exactly what Overwatch has become for me.

I return to it the way one returns to a familiar sin. Not because it is good for me, not because it rewards me, but because somewhere deep inside, I know what it can be. And that knowledge, like forbidden fruit, keeps me coming back.

This game is beautiful. Not just visually, but in motion. When everything clicks, when your team moves like a single organism, when abilities chain together with perfect timing, it feels almost divine. Like standing at the gates of something sacred. For a brief moment, you glimpse perfection.

And then it vanishes.

The ranked system feels less like a system and more like fate. You can play the game of your life, carry beyond reason, bend every mechanic to your will, and still fall. Other times you misplay, hesitate, do everything wrong, and somehow rise. It is not a ladder. It is a wheel that turns without explanation, indifferent to effort, blind to intention.

And I grind it anyway.

Match after match, like a pilgrim climbing a mountain that never ends. The rank progression promises structure and fairness, but behind it lies chaos. You are not climbing. You are circling, pushing the same weight uphill only to watch it roll back down.

And then there are the teammates.

Not always, but often enough to haunt every queue. Teammates who wander into fights alone as if drawn by some silent call. Teammates who refuse to group, who stagger in one by one like offerings. Supports who forget to heal, tanks who forget to create space, damage players who forget what damage even means. It feels like trying to conduct an orchestra where half the musicians cannot hear the music and the other half are playing a different song entirely.

You try to compensate. You overextend, overthink, overperform. You become more than one player because you have to be. And still it is not enough. The system does not care. It records the loss the same way it records every loss. Cold. Final. Unexplained.

And still, I queue again.

Because beneath all of it, beneath the frustration, the imbalance, the moments where the game feels actively against you, there is something undeniable. Not just addiction, but potential. The belief that the next match could be different. That the next team will understand. That the next game will finally reflect the effort you put in.

Sometimes it almost does.

A perfect push. A clean defense. A team that moves together without words, like they share the same mind. In those moments, the game becomes something more. Something rare. Something that feels earned.

And that is the curse of Overwatch.

It shows you heaven just long enough to make you tolerate everything else.

So I stay, caught between devotion and frustration, playing a game I cannot quit, trusting a system I do not understand, surrounded by teammates I cannot rely on, chasing a feeling that refuses to last.

I hadn’t played Overwatch for years, but recently I saw the new content, the addition of several heroes, and the skins being showcased on social media and YouTube. I’ve always loved this game, I own many skins (including mythics), and I decided to give it another chance.

However, my return has been overshadowed by a situation that honestly disappointed me.

I came across news and videos about the voice actress of a character (ANRA) asking for changes to the character’s design. Personally, I don’t understand the reason. This is a stylized, futuristic video game, not a realistic simulation. Part of the appeal of video games is experiencing worlds and characters that go beyond everyday reality.

The character, as it is, has a visually appealing design and fits perfectly within the game’s artistic style. A voice actress questioning the character’s appearance feels out of place. But what concerns me the most is not the opinion itself, but Blizzard publicly stating that they will consider redesigning the character.

As a long-time Blizzard player, it worries me to see the company responding to external pressures that are unrelated to gameplay, balance, or overall game quality. Inclusion and diversity are positive and natural in video games, and Overwatch has always done a great job in that regard. I have no issue with that.

The concern arises when creative decisions seem influenced by external debates or ideological trends rather than the artistic direction or the developers’ original vision.

Players come to enjoy the gameplay, the visual design, and the overall experience. Changing character aesthetics for reasons unrelated to the game itself creates uncertainty about the future direction of the project.

For these reasons, I’m leaving a negative review. It’s disappointing to say this because I’ve been a Blizzard fan for many years, but I’m increasingly concerned about the direction the company is taking.

Blizzard, keep it up like this: Marvel Rivals is going to eat your lunch.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

P.S. To everyone arriving here to defend the “offended” narrative — thank you for the emotional investment. It’s impressive how quickly some people mobilize over character aesthetics.

Peak player numbers are always worth celebrating. They’re also temporary. Hype is loud at the beginning. Time is quieter, and much more honest.

Marvel Rivals will still be here when the excitement fades and the charts return to normal. Trends come and go. Market reality tends to stay.

If this opinion bothers you, feel free to disagree internally. Not every reaction needs an audience.

Comments closed. Please direct further outrage elsewhere.

I've played Overwatch since the day it released on Xbox One and only briefly took a break around 2019. As with most of us it has always been a love hate relationship and you won't always like the decisions being made, but I can confidently say that in its current form, Overwatch is the best it has ever been. Gameplay and overall fun wise. 

They seem to be taking a lot more risks as of late and it has been paying off. With Stadium at first, perks, and an assortment of other changes such as hero release schedule and more. If you want just a solid fun experience this really cannot be topped when it comes to a hero shooter. 

My one huge negative is the way the game is monetized but I understand the game needs to make money in some way and skins just happen to be the one thing everyone can agree on for the most part. It does make us original players miss the way we could just play the game and unlock the skins we wanted for the characters we played. The level up system isn't as good as the original Overwatch and that's about my biggest gripe still. 

Not a huge fan of hero bans or map voting, I'd rather just maps be random as it takes less time getting into match without either of these. Also, I feel like you should have to learn how to counter characters you don't like playing against, not just flat out ban them, but to each their own. 

As I stated, this doesn't really take away from my enjoyment of the game and I feel like the team has done an outstanding job of righting the ship of what was the sinking Titanic of Overwatch 2.

Reading all the new negative reviews irked the ♥♥♥♥ out of me, so I might as well answer the common complaints. It says 4.1 hrs on steam, but I got like nearly 1k hrs on battle.net. TOPIC: the game is over hated for outdated reasons (alt title: people like to bandwagon cuz they cant form actual and insightful reviews)

Its previous CEO had a negative impact on the game, especially right before his resignation. So the negative reviews on Steam release were extremely valid as the game were at its all-time low (and the fresh taste of PvE cancellation, even tho none of yall complaining about it will ever actually play it past 30 min). The release of Marvel Rivals was an important competition for Blizzard to improve and be bold. The current dev team are trying to shake off the bad rep the game used to hold.

Now it's very over hated as it is in a much better state with obvious growing numbers (Stadium gamemode catering to more players, reworks and nice visual updates). And to say that the "2" was only meant for 'heavy micro transactions', how tf are they suppose to make money on a free game as a big studio? Sure it was egregiously bad earlier but now there are more discounts and free lootboxes (just ignore the money signs). Sure they said PvE was intended, but things fell flat and reusing story mode concepts into the PvP modes (like in arcade at the moment) to then officially adding perks is a great move. They now have lore info tab so read/watch those too with more coming!

also... YOU HAVE TO PLAY MORE TO PROGRESS MORE. It has more of a meaningful progression, especially for new players (and will continue to be). You'd be surprise with how much you can get rare shop/collabs or previous battlepass items for free just for playing. No need to worry about FOMO.

Hero balancing is understandably frustrating. With 45 heroes, and counting, being added there are bound for someone to counter someone. And with how they are adjusted constantly shifting the meta, at least it's not the same meta every day yk? Every hero is up for potential on the chopping block, or for a promotion, it gets you going. Definitely better now since release.

The matchmaking is all you expect out of an online team-based hero shooter. The ones complaining are the same ones that goes back to rivals with a much worse matchmaking. It isn't perfect. Sometimes you are good in one game, then you become the worse player on your team in the next. It happens. Mostly fueled by egotistical people with their life on the line into winning their games. It's gonna be a wider elo in unrank than on ranked (yes, unrank has a hidden skill rating so it caters roughly to your skill level). Think about who you are playing WITH. Anyone higher or lower than your skill rating is gonna have an effect in your matches, eventually favoring the higher skill = more harder games.

You can make hilarious and creative metaphors to negatively describe each aspect of the game, but it isn't accurate to how it's portrayed, at least in the present times rn. If you care about having a good team with comms, I would suggest discord servers or anything else adjacent (for ANY gamemode, mic or no mic). If you hate the 5v5 format, play 6v6. If you hate your mmr, strive to be better? seek a coach or watch videos and stop being ignorant.

Overwatch is a decent team-based shooter that has some great ideas but doesn’t always live up to its full potential. The game stands out because of its colorful cast of heroes, each with unique abilities that make matches feel different depending on team composition.

The gameplay can be really fun, especially when teams work well together. Abilities and ultimates create exciting moments, and the fast-paced matches keep things engaging. The art style and character design are also strong points, giving the game a lot of personality.

However, the experience can be inconsistent. Team coordination is very important, and if your team isn’t working together, matches can feel frustrating and one-sided. Balance issues and frequent changes can also make the game feel unstable at times.

Another downside is that it can get repetitive, especially if you play for long periods. Some players may also find the competitive side stressful rather than enjoyable.

Overall, Overwatch is an average game. It has fun mechanics and memorable characters, but issues with balance, teamwork, and repetition hold it back from being truly great.

If you ignore the absolute ♥♥♥♥♥ that this company has done to it's employees and all the failed promises that were never delivered, Overwatch 2 (yes, this is supposed to be the sequel to the completely fine and functioning "Overwatch" released in 2016 that they shut down, despite what the title may suggest) Is a pretty fun online shooter in my opinion. The cast of characters are all interesting and unique enough to cater to many different play style; there's only a few that I would consider kinda lame to play, which is pretty good considering how many characters there are. 

The story is cool enough to serve as a vessel for the characters to exist, but if your looking for anything beyond your typical superhero collaboration story, then you might want to skip it and play the game. That's not to say the story is bad. I actually think there are a lot of cool characters and ideals that are at play, even if the gameplay doesn't really reflect that. For instance, Vendetta, one of the newer characters introduced, has a really cool backstory which interelates with so many other characters and events in the Overwatch timeline, and results with (spoiler warning) her confronting Doomfist at the Talon headquarters where she literally disarms him and throws him out the window off a cliff, presumebly to his death, and usurps his role as the new head of Talon. This is a huge event in the story of Overwatch, which should have massive ramifications for its world. But this all gets kinda undercut when you load into a quickmatch and see Doomfist and Vendetta on the opposing team collaborating to assault your backline healers in jolly cooperation.

If I could ask one thing from this studio, it would be that it stops sexually harassing its employees (I think that's the first and biggest port of call before anything else proceeds). But after that, I would want the story of Overwatch to have a bigger impact on its gameplay. Make it interesting! I know we didn't actually see Doomfist die, which in pop-culture law means his death was ambiguous just in case they want to bring him back, but at least make it a bit more ambiguous! remove him from the roster for a season or two, just to make people think for a second that maybe, just maybe, Vendetta actually went and did it. And its not just the characters that could change. After overthrowing Doomfist, Vendetta, in her new position of power, launches an all out attack on Watchpoint Gibraltar, an online map in the game. But when you load into the map, virtually nothing about it has changed! This would have been a cool opportunity to change the map for a cool twist in the gameplay. Maybe a building gets knocked over to provid a new bit of cover, or a hole gets blown in the roof to provide a new shortcut or sightline for one of the flying characters. Give us something at least!

I know this is probably falling on deaf ears, but I really want the Blizzard team to do a little bit more to connect the story to it's game. The gameplay is good enough to keep me on for a little while, but if they really want me hooked on this franchise and to actually care about what's happening in its world, they need to make the story have more of an impact. There's definitely something there, they just need to execute on it. However until then, I'll happily hop on to play more Wuyang. I swear, whipping my little water orbs around corners in hopes of maybe eliminating someone is weirdly exhilerating lol.

I’ve been playing Overwatch since early 2018 and absolutely loved it for so many reasons. Fast forward to 2025, and after the disastrous launch of OW2 and all the surrounding drama, I can honestly say that in its current state, Overwatch 2 feels like the original, but with plenty of quality-of-life improvements, updates, and more content.

Earned loot boxes are back (and all your cosmetics carry over, of course), so I don’t feel any need to spend extra money to keep enjoying my mains. The game is frequently updated with new content and still includes nearly everything from the original (looking at you, CS2!), and retains that unique charm and gameplay I haven’t found in any other PvP shooter.

As someone who’s always played Overwatch casually rather than competitively, I find the community to be mostly positive, and the game consistently gives me a good time, more so than most multiplayer titles out there today.

All things considered, despite Blizzard’s past mistakes (and with "pro gamers" still finding reasons to complain), I genuinely think the devs deserve a second chance. The competition with Marvel Rivals seems to have pushed them in the right direction, and for the first time in years, I’m not only enjoying Overwatch again, I’m actually excited to see what comes next.

The game itself is "fine", runs pretty well, maps are visually readable and understandable, a lot of care has been taken to ensure that this game a serious competitive experience.

The issue becomes that there are so many smaller issues that spring up that make the game feel like a chore.

Some of these "chores" could be handled better, while some are a bit more difficult to handle.

Chore 1: the playerbase is a complete coinflip and any matches you get could either be completely fine, a really good time, or a total waste of everyone's time.

You can expect to get quite a wide selection of people for the matchmaking system to put you with and up against, except there's no component of said system that you're getting teammates worth playing with or opponents worth versing.

All it takes to completely mess up a whole competitive match (and casual by extention) is for some whiner to throw a tissy fit over quite literally nothing, put their teammates down, not contribute to helping the team, or to just be simply not playing at their best, and that can really harm the game's experience both in a competitive and casual context.

A lot of players are super likely to have a full on meltdown and having a bad mental state for your team just makes the situation worse, which a lot of players don't seem to understand, especially in lower ranks.

Chore 2: the game expects way too much coordination and cooporation between players and then punishes players for not coordinating or cooporating.

Sure, if people just used their common sense and learned to be aware, that system might help, but you can probably presume that it doesn't because not everyone is the most aware and common sense might not be so common depending on who you ask.

The game does not heavily consider this nor does it allow you to have any room for error, unless your opponents are making plenty of mistakes too, except that shouldn't be something that a team should be counting on.

That said, there are communication systems built into the game which let you communicate without needing to say a word and instead just use "pings" and "radio commands", but some people don't spend the time to learn how to use them and they are more likely to be missed instead of just typing or using voice chat.

The game really does try to make the game feel friendlier, but there's no system in place to keep the irritants away from people who actually want to play the game outside of a "just report them" mentality.

From experience, both low and high ranks can have completely competent players and it is more than possible to play both with and around your teammates, but some players make it actually impossible.

Chore 3: the ranking system doesn't help either, it's in dire need of a rework and the whole wide-queue system for 5v5 is helpful when you do have lower ranked friends you want to play competitive with, but it is almost never worth it given that all it takes is for one player to completely ruin your chances at any potential team fights or holding a point.

6v6 is a lot better to play in both competitive and casual contexts and it being "open queue" (as in you can pick your own roles, in case you didn't know) makes it way easier to pick up the slack if another player is struggling.

5v5 overall is a lot harder and a lot less enjoyable, it can be a good time, but you really do need a good team or you will get stuck in what ends up being a complete and utter waste of time 90% of the you enter the matchmaking queue.

It's too easy to get skill-gapped teams where one team is just better than the other, the gamemodes are either boring or tedious, with payload maps being by far the best and flashpoint being by far the worst, everything else exists between those two.

The lack of new maps and modes being brought in makes playing the same thing over and over again lame (even though a new map is being brought in soon at the time of writing), especially considering a heap of older modes are still in the game, but are just not playable in competitive but are stupidly instead "limited time gamemodes" with casual settings.

In any case, the game has its highlights and is overall a much better experience today than it was in 2016, but the modes, maps, matchmaking, and even the balancing needs some serious effort put in to releave some of the stresses, otherwise you could easily sink a good couple hundred hours into this game if you find it's your thing.

Given it's free, it may be worth trying out anyway, just be ready to spend a good while learning the mechanics before you find yourself actually having fun.

Rant alert: 
The game and gameplay isn't even the bad part. It runs smoothly and feels and looks nice. Also feels fairly balanced at this point. The matchmaking and teammates though. I have >200h on this now on PC and Xbox. That is like a thousand games. Out of those games, maybe 20 (being VERY generous in this estimation), I have not carried hard. Like HARD. Even in ranked it's the same. Constantly carrying the dumbest brainlets the universe managed to create, only to once in a while completely wipe the enemy team with absolutely no resistance. Even the games that feel even actually aren't. You win, think "I did well there", look at the scoreboard and realize that you are so far ahead of everyone in the entire lobby that it would be funny if it wasn't the case every game, and that the ENTIRE enemy team has double the stats of all your other teammates. 

Like I never thought I would say this but I would enjoy this game more with SBMM probably if it gave me teammates with a brain. 
Also, I'm like level 30 as Orisa, my most played character, at 150h on PC, and these teammates are often double, triple or even above 5 times my level and are still making no noticeable impact in any way. 

I have also gone back and reviewed my old game replays to check if my memory is biased with heat of the moment- emotion. It is not. Literally my friend and I are the only sentient beings on our team almost every game. Only ones on objective, first ones going into the fights, last ones to die while dealing the most damage, getting the most kills (or heals if we're healing) and singlehandedly taking care of all the enemy healers while our own DPS don't shoot at the tanks' shields because they're scared they'll run out of ammo or something.

So yeah basically, having teammates that have 500+ hours and still don't know that shields break if shot or that multiple people shooting the same enemy drains their health faster and out- damages heals, ie. all the stuff that normal people learn in 10 minutes of playing- if that ♥♥♥♥ (among many, oh so many other things) pisses you off and you don't want to sweat your ass off unless you want to lose every game, this game is not for you. 

If you want to run around the map, shoot at things every now and then, fight and run away, try different abilities or look at your cool skins, this game is definitely for you.

Alright, so Overwatch 2 is the sequel (or kind of a major update) to the original Overwatch — that team-based shooter that’s all about picking heroes with unique abilities and working together to push payloads or capture points.

First off: visually, it looks sharper and more polished. The maps are fresh, the heroes got some tweaks, and the game runs smoother overall. It still has that colorful, vibrant style that makes it easy to jump into and figure out what’s going on even in the heat of battle.

Gameplay-wise, it’s classic Overwatch but with a few new twists — like the switch to 5v5 (down from 6v6), which really changes the pace and team dynamics. Matches feel a bit faster and more focused, which is cool if you’re into more intense, tactical fights.

The hero roster has expanded, with some interesting new characters that bring fresh playstyles. Plus, all the originals are still there, so you’ve got tons of options no matter what kind of player you are.

That said, Overwatch 2 feels like more of an evolution than a revolution. If you played the first game a lot, you’ll feel right at home, but if you were hoping for a totally fresh experience, it might not blow your mind. It’s a polished package with some new goodies, but the core is very familiar.

The free-to-play model means anyone can jump in, but beware — cosmetics, skins, and other goodies can get pricey if you want the flashiest look. Also, the game’s competitive scene is still finding its feet, with some balancing issues and matchmaking hiccups here and there.

Bottom line: if you loved Overwatch, this is a solid next step — smoother, faster, and still fun. If you’re new to the series, it’s a great time to jump in, but don’t expect a complete overhaul of the formula.

A once unique experience that got ruined for a live-service model that ruined the user experience. I'll just leave my thoughts below after 2k hours since the Overwatch 1 beta days. 

- Matchmaking is down right awful. I don't know why the matchmaking in this game feels like it needs to punish solo Q players. No I don't always blame my team for losses. I know it sometimes my fault that I could have played better, but why does Blizzard find it acceptable to match me against the same 3-6 stack every match? If you solo Q and there happens to be a stack in your region you're going to see them non-stop in competitive. The matchmaking does not stop for a second to think, "Mhhh this guy has played against this same group for 3 matches in a row and we keep giving him the same random team, surely we should look for an alternative match? Nah 4th time is the charm." Getting placed on the apposite side of a 3-6 stack team wouldn't be such a bad thing if people decided to use mics and communicate with each other. But this is Overwatch in 2025 were talking about, everyone who plays this game is anti-social and has social anxiety.  Just saying a simple "Hello" in VC is such a daunting task for anyone in this game that they'd rather get backshots from the enemy stack than communicating. But when things go bad everyone types 500 Words per minute and flame the tank and DPS. If 10% of this community applied themselves in any other aspect in their life I am convinced we would solve most modern problems like a cure for cancer, world hunger, and global conflict. But these degenerates rather be spending 12 hours a day bad mouthing other players for a singular mistake and never look in the mirror. Oh and Mercy players... you all ain't cute and think your the main character because you hold left click and occasionally fly around pressing shift. I hate you the most because you contribute nothing and blame everyone else for your failures.  

- Stadium caters to all the characters that sell skins and leave them broken for months on end until. They nerfed Freja in less than a week after being put in stadium but continue to let a character like D.va have the ability of having a broken dumb builds is beyond annoying. Which one do you think is selling more skins? 

- The dev team preaches about creating a safe-space for the community, but over the last 5-6 months in my ranked matches I see p*do jokes, racism, sexism, and even had a couple instances of hearing terms thrown at minority groups in VC. You'd think they get banned right? Nah I still see them in my diamond-low master lobbies. But they banned a guy for saying "Noob." There's the morale compass this team believes in, only after getting called out did they deem "Noob" was fine like all the other racist, sexism, and other stuff I hear and read in game. The report system barely works and has been known to wrongfully ban players with Blizzard support basically telling them "Sorry you're out of luck." Oh and its perfectly fine to brag about smurfing in this game, the devs and report system don't really care. Only way action gets taken against players is when mass-reporting happens to an account and the automated system does the job blizzard is to lazy to do. If a stack of players decide to report you than your account gets flagged and eventually can lead to an undeserved ban. 

- Balancing philosophy is stupid, I'm convinced there's not a single person on the dev team who is above silver 5. They hear about broken characters and just change very minor things like -/+ .5 seconds off an ability and never address why a character is broken or annoying to play against. Kiri hardly ever gets touched because you can't touch the golden children in this game...

- The skin economy is beyond stupid in this game. You can guarantee three characters are going to get skins every season. Kiri, Juno, and Mercy. Not just a "Skin" no I mean "Skins." You like a certain character that isn't gooner friendly? Well I hope you enjoy grinding loot boxes for some old OW1 cosmetics and OW2 season passes skins so that you can MAYBE get that characters skin. This dev team better be thankful for all the gooners this community has because allot of them would be homeless and out of a job. (Just look at Mercy's persona collab skin.) Oh and if your favorite character doesn't sell skins then they'll never buff them to make them as viable as other characters. Kiri never gets touched despite the community wanting changes to her Suzu, Damage, and TP. She sells so its fine to leave her in a unbalanced way. 

- If a person who enjoys Overwatch hears you say "They didn't deliver on any of the promises they said about OW2" they'll tell ya to move on. Honestly, you guys are whats wrong about live-service games. I spent money for the original release of the game in OW1 and it got taken away from people like me who actually misses the developers pouring their actual love into this franchise. Now you're just a wallet for them to keep scamming. Yeah Jeff could have made the transition easier, but you can't tell me OW2 isn't a downgrade from OW1 in its current state. 

- They introduced a ban and map voting system. While I think this is the one positive change this game has done, lets not kid ourselves and say this wasn't because of RIvals bringing competition to the market. They could've done this earlier and implemented features the community has been wanting for years, but all is forgiven because now it has a direct competitor? Nahhhhhh its scummy and insulting. This game was printing money because every game that tried to compete with them sucked and there wasn't a decent alternative. 

- Every new character they come out with has little to no soul attached to them. The last two characters they came out with that had any semblance of being cool and unique was Ramattra and Junker Queen. Actual characters that had some story and awesome design. Since then? 

[list]
    [*]Kiriko - A cliche anime shaped character with a back story that contradicts what little lore this game has. 
    [*]Lifeweaver - A hyper flirtatious guy with roses...
    [*]Illari - Such a fumbled character, an interesting premise and back story that they never expanded because she doesn't sell.
    [*]Mauga - Hey lets put Dwayne "The Rock" Johnson in our game... oh he said no? Give him two miniguns and some hair so people wont notice.
    [*]Venture - They eat rocks, isn't that funny guys? Lets forget they ever existed and not throw her skins for a few seasons... takes away resources for our female characters.
    [*]Juno - Another generic anime figure who lacks any social awareness. Oh she's an astronaut? Lets make every new skin for her non space-themed and more gooner friendly. 
    [*]Hazard - Oh people really liked the concept artwork before this new dev team took over? Nah that's to original. Just make him generically buff with spikes. 
    [*]Freja - Another boring designed character. But this time lets give her a personality that matches her design. 
    [*]Wuyang - Honestly just feels like the dev team said "Lets take Juno and Kiriko and just make them into a guy." His kit is unique I'll give them that, but like majority of the new characters made its just another ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ boring anime inspired character. 
[/list] 

Yes this game is addicting, but its really hindered by a greedy dev team that doesn't really care about making it better. Only reason we saw changes the last year is because another game decided to copy its formula and do somethings better and a group of the community wanting to jump ship. Preface: This review is from the POV of someone who only plays tank and no other roles. I used to play this game on battlenet where I have around 1500 hours in total. 

Tanking has always been one of the least popular roles in video games, but i think tanking in overwatch 2 especially is the least fun compared to other games. Go look at online discussions/forums/videos/etc about this game and you'll easily find other people who share the same sentiment. Here are my own reasons as to why I dont recommend this game as a tank player:


1. The shift from 2 tanks to 1 tank inherently means you carry the most burden in your team, dps and support both have 2 players so when one is underperforming, the other one can pick up the slack. 

2. Because you're tanking by yourself and other roles still have 2 players each, this leads to a big amount of toxicity towards the tank. One person can say "tank gap" and everyone else can easily pile on that to cover their own gameplay and instantly have a scapegoat. Other roles both have 2 players so they can back each other up when the blamefest happens, but they know it's easiest to just point their finger at someone who's playing alone. 

3. The playerbase for this game can be really toxic at times, im not sure why but there's something about this game that brings out the worst in people. Even other online games aren't like this. Be ready to get blamed for everything that happens, if something slightly goes wrong then you'll be the first to blame, people are really quick to blame and point fingers in this game. 

4. Due to the nature of tanking, you'll always be on the frontline, everyone else will be hyperfocused on you which means everything that you do will always be highlighted. When you made a mistake everyone will see, which is different from other roles such as support who can see everyone because they're always at the back, and if they made a mistake it won't be as apparent. Which is why from my experience the flaming usually comes from the supports first.

5. Even when you're playing well, you're not exempt from being blamed, im sure every tank players have experienced being blamed when they are doing well, meanwhile other roles are very obviously underperforming. For example i had a game where we were losing, i was around 29-10 and my dps were 13-9 and 9-9, and of course the supports blamed me. Point is be ready to take all the blame all the time. 

6. Even if we put the social aspects of tanking aside, tanking in itself doesn't very fun because you always feel like the punching bag for everyone. All the CCs will be thrown at you, and not to mention the insufferable counterswapping. I prefer playing heroes that i like, butt when the entire enemy team counterswap you, at that point it's really hard unless you swap to a different hero. This goes on and on. Your teammates will constantly order you to "tank play this hero, tank play that hero" but they often don't help you to counterswap the enemy team. 

7. I feel like the only time tanking feels fun is when your teammates are better than the enemy team, so you don't have to be sweating your balls off to carry and you can just kinda chill. Though it feels redundant to say because obviously winning is fun, but that's about the only moment that i can think of where tanking does feel fun. 

Will probably update this review later, still have some stuff i wanna say but i cant think of them right now. But you probably get the point by now

Overwatch 2 is, in my opinion, still one of the best hero shooters on the market right now. People claiming that Marvel Rivals is better are often just stuck in lower ranks and haven’t really mastered the game.

The game from Blizzard Entertainment feels extremely polished, with smooth gameplay and responsive controls. It’s easy to pick up for beginners, but mastering different heroes, team compositions, and positioning takes real skill and time.

One of the biggest strengths is the hero design — every character feels unique, with distinct abilities and playstyles. This makes matches feel fresh and encourages experimenting instead of sticking to just one role.

There’s also a wide variety of maps and objectives, which keeps gameplay from getting repetitive. Regular updates, balance changes, and seasonal events help keep the game alive and evolving.

The Battle Pass system is very accessible. Weekly challenges stack, so even if you don’t play consistently, you can still catch up and complete it without feeling forced to grind every day.

Another big plus is the strong team-based gameplay. Communication and coordination actually matter, which makes winning feel rewarding and satisfying.

Performance-wise, the game is well-optimized and runs smoothly even on mid-range PCs, which makes it accessible to a wider audience.

And of course, it’s free-to-play, so there’s no risk in trying it yourself instead of relying on negative opinions online.

Overwatch features a profanity filter that you can toggle on or off, but auto-bans you for swearing: I called out Blizzard support after my second ban and they admitted it.

This game draws you in for the first 50ish levels by putting you against people who play the game like normal and don't spend 24/7 in a basement spreading cheeto dust all over their mouse and keyboards. Sadly, past that mark that is all that you will run into, at least for enemy teams. The game has really cool characters but after a certain point it becomes ruined by people who play the game *too* much and matchmaking simply does not care (All modes even quick play). I avoided this game for the longest because I knew the stereotype of people who play this game, then was pleasantly surprised when I tried it and wasn't like that. But like I said, only for the first 50 levels - then matchmaking matches you against people who 4 or 5 stack and collectively don't leave their basements. Such a bad fanbase. Absolutely ruins the game and any fun you're trying to have. 

Also - I thought this game was pretty well balanced, for the most part. But, after playing for a bit, you start to see legacy characters like Hanzo and Genji who's skill ceiling is not limited AT ALL ruin the game. Yes you should be rewarded for being good at a character, but they are the only one's capable of repeatedly one shotting the player which creates a very infuriating experience. Also not to mention the hacking problem. Being skillful with these characters is more than rewarding just because they were some of the first and it's forbidden to adjust them apparently. However, for those not skillful with them, Blizzard has a pretty much non-existent anti cheat system and in-game cheat detection just does not exist. 

If you are going to play this game I would recommend only playing it until the 50 level mark and not spending money on it either because the prices are outrageous. TLDR I see why this game was dead for years now and is now digging it's own grave once again.

The matchmaking system is extremely questionable. After a strong performance and a win, the system consistently places you into "uphill battles” in an apparent attempt to drag your overall win rate back toward 50%.
The developers claim that their philosophy is based on fairness and that, ideally, both teams should have an equal chance of victory. I agree with that principle. However, what is actually implemented bears little resemblance to it. Rather than creating fair matches, the system appears to react to wins by stacking the odds against the player in subsequent games.
In practice, this means being paired with significantly weaker teammates after a victory and facing opponents with far greater experience, coordination, and playtime. The result is not competitive balance, but repeated, one sided stomps that are frustrating rather than challenging. Because no matchmaking system can perfectly predict outcomes, attempting to “force” a 50% win rate in this way only exposes the system’s flaws.
This approach does not promote fairness. It promotes volatility, frustration, and a sense that performance is being punished rather than rewarded. Competitive matches become less about skill and more about whether the system has decided it is time for you to lose.
It is therefore unsurprising that the community often turns toxic. The environment created by this matchmaking design breeds resentment and hostility rather than healthy competition.
At its core, this feels like a lazy and misguided solution to a complex problem. Instead of refining true skill based matchmaking, the system resorts to crude corrective measures that degrade the player experience. Until the developers acknowledge these shortcomings and meaningfully address them, I cannot recommend investing time or money into the game.

Overwatch is not a game I enjoy. It is a toxic little ritual I perform every evening because apparently my brain has mistaken emotional damage for entertainment. I will finish a match furious, swear I am uninstalling, explain to everyone in Discord that this game is a diseased social experiment made by people who hate happiness, and then immediately queue again because maybe this match will be different. It will not be different. I will pick a character I barely understand, contribute approximately one useful ability every six minutes, and then become personally offended when my team does not carry me like the fragile porcelain disappointment that I am. Every loss feels rigged, every win feels undeserved, and every Play of the Game is footage of me dying in the background while somebody else experiences joy.

The worst part is I do not even know what I am chasing anymore. A rank? Validation? The brief spiritual high of eliminating someone who annoyed me? I have yelled “this game is garbage” while reopening it before the defeat screen even finished loading. My relationship with Overwatch is no longer player and video game; it is more like a raccoon returning to the same tipped-over trash can every night, fully aware there is nothing good inside, but emotionally incapable of walking away. I hate the balance, I hate the matchmaking, I hate every hero that kills me, I hate most of the heroes I play, and most importantly, I hate that I will be back tomorrow. Rated 2 broken keyboards out of 5 — unfortunately I’ll be putting in another shift tonight.

Overwatch Review (After My Sanity Has Left the Building)

I installed Overwatch thinking I was about to play a fun team-based shooter. What I actually downloaded was a 40-gigabyte psychological experiment designed to test how long a human being can endure chaos before they start questioning reality.

First of all, the matchmaking system clearly operates on a complex algorithm called **“flip a coin and ruin someone’s evening.”** One game your team is a perfectly coordinated squad of tactical geniuses. The next game you are grouped with what appears to be three goldfish, a microwave, and a guy who thinks the objective is optional.

And speaking of objectives, Overwatch players treat the payload like it’s a cursed artifact. Nobody wants to touch it. I will be sitting there alone pushing the cart like a minimum wage employee while my entire team is somewhere across the map fighting a lone enemy Genji who is doing backflips and screaming in Japanese.

Which brings me to Genji players.

Genji players do not play Overwatch. They are performing **a spiritual ritual.** They double jump, triple jump, wall climb, dash, deflect, spin, scream “I NEED HEALING” 400 times, and then die instantly. I have seen hummingbirds with less movement.

Meanwhile the enemy Widowmaker has been staring at the same pixel for 17 minutes and the moment I peek my pinky toe around a corner my entire skeleton leaves my body.

The balance in this game is also fascinating. Some heroes require advanced strategy, perfect aim, and a PhD in physics to use correctly. Others involve holding one button while the enemy team explodes like popcorn in a microwave.

And let’s talk about team communication.

Overwatch voice chat is like attending a family Thanksgiving dinner where everyone hates each other. You have:

* One guy who sounds like he hasn’t slept since 2007
* A 12-year-old screaming tactical instructions he learned from TikTok
* Someone breathing directly into their microphone like a Darth Vader cosplay
* And one mysterious silent player who suddenly types “gg ez” after contributing absolutely nothing

The ranked system is also a masterpiece of emotional damage. You win five games and climb one millimeter. You lose one match because your tank decided to explore the map like a tourist and suddenly the game demotes you to **“Cardboard 3.”**

But the most impressive feature of Overwatch is how it convinces you to keep playing.

You will lose.
You will suffer.
You will question your life choices.

And then the game will give you **one** good match. One perfect match where your team actually coordinates, the ult combos work, and everything feels amazing.

And your brain goes:
“See? This is fun. Let’s play another.”

Four hours later you are staring at the defeat screen while a Lucio wallrides past your corpse playing the world’s smallest violin.

In conclusion, Overwatch is a beautifully animated circus where everyone is on fire, the clowns have rocket launchers, and the ringmaster is laughing while the servers burn.

10/10 would absolutely install again because apparently I hate myself.

Incredible. The community, the pacing, and the overall match experience is like none other. Overwatch is truly a special game, and will always hold a sentimental place in my heart. For this, I have given it this not recommended rating, as it has mentally altered me.

This is a review coming from someone whos first hero shooter was marvel rivals. As of the posting of this season 7 pretty much just released, so i'm just gonna explain my thoughts on how this game feels as a rivals player.

Firstly, this game feels like complete ♥♥♥♥. I'm a support player, I almost always play supports in games. I dont exclusively healbot or anything, I try to play agressive with my team and match their pace in general. Every. Single. Support in this game feels awful. Healing in general feels awful. I've never had a single case with any support that I've played that I've been able to out-heal their damage. You would think, like normal hero shooters, that a 2 people (one dps + one support) would almost always win against one person (one dps). That is NOT AT ALL THE CASE IN THIS GAME. You CANNOT rely on your healing to heal you or your ally, the enemy will ALWAYS out-damage any form of healing that your able to do, regardless of the numbers advantage. I had a game where there were two support ultimates on a single tank, and that tank STILL died to a SINGLE DPS. 

Secondly, the matchmaking is completely awful. I played my first game, got completely steam rolled. I was like "Okay, bad luck i guess, lets try again". Same thing. I've gone on 8-9 game loss streaks, and any games that i *do* win are by a hair. I've never had a "definitive" win in this game. Eventually i figured out why, because every single game i was playing I was going against green, purple, or gold "badge" people (whatever the symbol is called that you can display showing how much you've played a character). I started marking down what I saw, and and I was primarily playing with people between *level 250 to level 500*. SUPPOSEDLY this game has skill based match making. It clearly does not, I dont know why the game just blatantly lies to you but it does.

Okay, so my main role plays awful and cant do its job correctly. I cant learn the game because i get completely steam rolled when playing quickmatch. So... what do i do? I was told by a friend that this game is in the best state that its ever been in. Is that just completely wrong? This game feels awful to play. I even tried DPS's and Tanks just to try and get a feel for how much damage would be incoming and how to prepare for it, but every DPS requires so much mechanical skill (which i cant learn because i get steamrolled the moment i peek around a corrner), and every tank feels somehow overpowered and underpowered at the same time. Like they can do way more damage than the should be able to, but even with that increased health, they die almost instantly.

I've left comments open because I genuinely want to know what im missing here. As of writing this, overwatch has about ~27k less players online, and ~33k less players within a 24 hour peak. So, if overwatch is so good, why do the numbers say otherwise? why is rivals still and seemingly objectively the better game? I've been told that the reviews for this game arent right because it got review bombed, and I know that it got review bombed, but genuinely after playing this game, it kinda deserves the rating that its at.

You know what? The game is fun again, these guys are finally reacting to the rest of the industry and player feedback. QoL and the game's systems are on par with its contemporaries, heroes are fun to play, and Blizzard is much more generous than they used to be as you get rewarded for just playing more consistently, hero progression is a nice touch and the new way loot boxes are used is a welcome addition. (I will say that the game tells you the legendary rate is 5%, which is much much better than pretty much any gacha out there, but there's so much garbage in the loot pool that it might as well feel like a 1% chance anyway)

There are still some other growing pains and some mistakes, unfortunately. For one, the new Conquest seasonal event is riddled with FOMO, as the challenges (and loot boxes) expire weekly, and it's impossible to fill the conquest pass in a single weekend, so I guess go F yourself if you're a weekend warrior like myself. Another thing I really wish Blizzard would commit too is to keep selling old battle passes, let's be real, we're only seeing this resurgence mostly because Marvel Rivals was such a threat to Overwatch, and there are other games that still make older passes available. FOMO is just not acceptable these days (not like it ever was, but alas).
I have issues with the matchmaker as well, I'm fairly certain it's not working right. For example there's the ranked modifiers that change how much you progress your comp rank, it makes no sense to me. Why are you putting me in a match expecting me to lose, or to steam roll? If I'm playing with people with the same skill as me, there shouldn't be this kind of bias.

There's also the subject of Stadium, but I can't speak too much on it as I'm completely repulsed by traditional MOBAs. I bought this game full price back in 2016 so I wouldn't have to play DOTA or League after all. 

Regardless, I think Aaron and the team has done a bang up job keeping the lights on beneath the iron fist of  Activision/Microsoft. It's not ideal, but I have hope the 2016 game of the year makes it's way back to our good graces.

The truth is Blizzard did nothing but lie about their PVE, save their PR on some scandals, and then release a nightmare mess of a game. I have so much to complain about: the microtransactions, the unusual balance changes, the new unforgettable character designs (Who is Wuyang???) and their inability to fit into the overall game dynamic.

But most of these would be understandable if the matchmaking wasn't a nightmare.
I understand this game is F2P and there are a large amount of people playing at once. However -- and I cannot stress this enough -- there is no excuse for the lack of voice chat. 
I haven't played this game since 2019. I hop back in just to have some fun. 3 comp games in and the game automatically puts me in silver. 30 games in and there is not a single person with chat or voice chat, except for one person who did not even speak English. In fact, I played a game where my attempts at calling out a flanking hero was useless, because no one was in voice chat or text chat.

I'm sorry, but there is no pro player on Earth right now that can play with 4 or 5 blind teammates and make it out of any sort of elo hell. This game heavily relies on communication for the healing, the ability coordination, and the callouts. There is no way in hell this game should allow matchmaking with extremely limited communication, and then punish the player by placing them in a lower rank. 

I understand that this is a common problem in multiplayer games. Somehow, Marvel Rivals, Paladins, and TF2 all have at least one person who has a means of communication every game I've played.
There MUST be a way to incentivize communication in the game without me HAVING to get 4-5 friends on every single time I want to play.

This is the second-oldest good Hero shooter; the first one is Team Fortress 2. The only problem this game has is that it’s created by Blizzard, the worst anti-consumer company you can imagine. After the Marval Rival launched, this game has suffered in comparison as Rival is a successor to what the OW2 was supposed to be for Overwatch. But Blizzard turns down their anti-consumer tricks a little less to get more playerbase, which I give them some credit for. I joined OW2 in the first session and saw their predatory monetization when there was no actual good competitor. I left after the first session, but loved the core gameplay. I have joined S18 to see why everyone is saying it's not that bad anymore. People and Stremer are right, it’s in a way better state, but not as good as the Marvel Rival Level in monetization, but going in a good direction. If you can ignore the monetization part or just want to play for free, it’s a good game, but I like to support games that I like, so it’s decent for me at the moment. 

[b] A few things they can do to fix the monetization - [/b]

[/list]

I've been playing this game since 2017, with almost 2,000 hours under my belt, and I switched to Steam in November 2025 after switching to Linux Fedora 43 without any problems.

Say what you will, this game is almost 10 years old, and it pains me to say it, but the switch to F2P has definitely saved the game. Despite the PVE debacle and the overpriced shop, there are regular updates every season, with new maps and heroes.

The gameplay has evolved well and remains excellent, with perks and the stadium adding new ways to play and new team strategies. The ping system and action wheel are basic but sufficient to avoid having to use the microphone in quick matches.

The developers seem to be more responsive to players with regular surveys (at least two in 2025), regular in-game tests over several days, hero rebalancing that is much more responsive than in OW1, and finally the return of loot boxes.

The transition to F2P has not been without its hiccups, as there is more toxicity, a few hackers (who are reported without mercy), and the infamous shop with its horribly expensive Kiriko skins.
I'm content with a few points via Microsoft Rewards to at least get the Battle Pass and a free shop skin for Mei or Symmetra each season or collaboration.

There are worse things and there may be better things, but OW2 seems to have found a balance, so long may it last.

Alright, let's actually provide a constructive review. Why are people "review bombing" Overwatch 2?
    
We paid for Overwatch, we supported it throughout its lifespan, it was popular and liked by its community, and turning a happy profit each year.
 
When Blizzard announced Overwatch 2, the big new feature they announced, the entire premise that made it "worth the sequel treatment" was the PvE campaign mode. Hero talent trees, customizable abilities, an expansive co-op campaign that would add to the story and lore. We were told it was going to be so much work that they wouldn't be putting out updates for Overwatch any more, they were re-allocating the staff to work on this new project.

With no updates, and no content, Overwatch started to get stale... people grew tired and bored, spending less time in the game, or moving on. Many of us stuck with the product, playing a little each week just to keep fresh... waiting for the new campaign mode. For a lot of us, playing became a chore, something we were doing out of habit, so we'd be ready for the new campaign...

But the new campaign wasn't the premise that established OW2, it was the pretext. It was a lie. Blizzard abandoned the PvE campaign a full YEAR before OW2 launched - but continued to push it in marketing as the big new feature. It's even in the announcement trailer for OW2, and on the roadmaps. They knew they'd canned it 12 months earlier, but they knew that's what people wanted, so kept putting it front and centre to get people's money, to get people to buy into the project. They took our trust and monetized it.

-----

What does OW2 ACTUALLY offer then? What did they work on for those two years that was so important that they couldn't make content for OW1, the product people paid for and were supporting?

1) They overhauled the graphical engine:

Before, Overwatch would play on a relatively low spec computer, and it'd be smooth. Now, it stutters and stammers in the menus. They've added this weird shiny plastic finish to everything in the world, heroes and environment alike - so where before, heros would "pop" against the background and be easy to follow, now everything has the same texture to it, and it all merges together into visual noise - especially at longer ranges. They've compensated for how bad this looks by adding lots of additional flashy effects and explosions, that makes it much harder to see anything amidst the chaos. A PvP game at this pace relies heavily on quick disambiguation of what you're looking at, so you can react and compete, and the new engine makes this impossible without turning all the effects back off... meaning that the two years of development is for a feature best disabled.

The new engine also has a weird slippery "feel" to it. Reviewers who'd played Overwatch for 50 hours came into Overwatch 2 and reported "look! it's the same Overwatch you love, but it's F2P now! Just ignore the monetization and you can play this great game for free!" Established long term players with thousands of hours were on reddit complaining that their hitboxes were all over the place and shots that would previously "feel" like a dead certain hit were just completely whiffing, and they didn't understand why. All the timings are messed up.

2) They reworked most of the heroes:

TLDR: they screwed up the game balance. Previously, one of the biggest appeals of Overwatch was that you could find a hero that suited your play style, and main them, getting good at whatever suited you best. Some people like sniping, or hitscan run and gun, some people aren't good at reactionary aim and instead liked to focus on positioning, tactics and strategy. You could make a big positive different to your team by playing lucio or mercy, and paying attention to your surroundings, and positioning yourself in the right place at the right time to make the difference.

With Overwatch 2, the damage and healing behaviour has been reworked heavily. Now, the one tank on the team is insanely chunky, and it's near impossible to kill them quickly - with healer support, they can just stand in the line of fire and laugh. Everyone else dies almost immediately, regardless of who their aggressor is.

Team-fighting now is very one-note, you have one tank who's immortal if they have support, and you have four hitscan DPS characters following them - there's not a lot of difference between supports and DPS characters. The teams try to pick off the other team's DPS/Support characters, and when they pick a few off, they can finish the tank and take the objective. Repeat. There's no room for variation in play style at all, you either play a DPS and try to kill the other team DPS, or you play a tank, and try to position correctly - maybe picking off an enemy hero that gets too close to you.

I understand a lot of this came from feedback at the professional levels, where support players were complaining that they wanted to have more direct impact in teamfights, they wanted the game to feel more like other FPS games - but for a large portion of Overwatch's playerbase specifically, the appeal of the game was that you could do something else and still matter.

3. They got INCREDIBLY greedy with the monetization...

It's a longstanding argument that lootboxes are terrible in modern gaming... but Overwatch was one of the few examples of Lootboxes done right. Most of the long-term players of the game actually liked the system, that just rewarded you for playing the game and let you unlock whatever content you wanted relatively quickly. I started playing OW1 with the first anniversary, cleared the weekly boxes from arcade every week, and at the launch of OW2, I owned every single item in the game. Those who didn't have the time to invest in doing everything could still quickly unlock specific items they wanted (and let's be honest, when you have your favourite skin, how much do you care about unlocking other skins?) - and those who didn't want to spend hours unlocking stuff could spend a little cash to get it - if they wanted to.

The new system puts almost everything behind a paywall. The minute number of cosmetics on the "free" track are often insultingly lazy, simple recolours or minor alterations, while they put serious effort into skins that you can only buy for $20. The content you can unlock by grinding often feels like it only exists to drag you into interfaces that advertise the paid content for you. Even content from the first game, that was originally free, is now paid only.

Battlepass systems suck. "Pay us for the opportunity to unlock stuff if you play a lot. If you don't you have to pay us more, or lose the stuff you already paid for." - Limited time deals, rotating shop offers, FOMO FOMO FOMO... it reeks of manipulation and insincerity.

4. Occasional DLC packs of PvE adventures.

These are Blizzard's "new" approach to PvE. Occasional mission packs where you can get a little bit of story and a PvE mission. You can pay (in my UK currency) £13 for the DLC pack, or £35 (the cost of a good indie game" for the "ultimate bundle".

So here's the thing - this content is the same amount of content, and the same pace of release as the old mission scenarios - it's just now, instead of them being a free event to get people excited about playing and encourage their friends to join up - now they're paid DLC.

Nobody likes paying a regular subscription for a product that they previously owned completely. It doesn't make anyone like Adobe Photoshop or Microsoft Office, and it doesn't make anyone like Blizzard.

So this is why people are "review bombing" your game. You took something we paid for, supported and enjoyed for years, lied to us about your plans, and turned it into trash. Now you're telling us to pay for features and content we previously enjoyed in the game we paid for. It's greedy, it's cynical, it's manipulative, and you've lost our trust.

Also you should fire Bobby Kotick.

Long time Overwatch player. It pains me to write this but this OW2 game is a sad excuse for Overwatch, There is a long list of things they have done wrong that I may detail below but generally this game had so much heart and ingenuity in OW1 but they literally gutted that game in the sequel to make way for 20.00$ skins and gouging of fans at every angle. I want to like OW2 but they lost my respect when they stopped seeing their players as patrons and more as cash cows.

OW2 isn't review bombed, it deserves it's rating. The gameplay loop is mostly the same as it always was which will remain to be great. but everyone behind the scenes is concerned only with profit now at the expense of the players/patrons. 2/10
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
for a more in depth breakdown I will detail a exhaustive list of the many faults ive seen with OW2 until the present after 100's of hours gameplay between both games: 
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
-OW2 was marketed to players as a game that would have a great single/multiplayer story mode and was the focus of the sequel, after 2 years in development they decided to trash the story mode and skill trees and release ow2 without either, a HUGE slap to the face of players.

-OW2 developers decided that 6v6 was problematic and spent a huge amount of time reworking the game to 5v5 format. they spent a ton of time on this and essentially dropped the game cutting 6v6 entirely and ultimately making the gameplay less dynamic and with less players in each match and forcing solo tanking (yes double shield was real but thoughtful players could easily play around it)

-OW2 removed lootboxes from the game to replace it with a battlepass system. a system that was emerging all over games at the time. but what people may not remember is; they gave in-game currency in lootboxes, now in battlepass they removed earning of in-game currency & it wasn't until season 4-5 that they even added currency kickbacks in the battlepass to refund some cost like almost all major battlepasses do.

-OW2 virtually Eliminated earning of in-game currency, now 2000 coin skins could really only feasibly be acquired thru purchase, and they did have earnable currency but only if you completed ALL WEEKLY CHALLENGES which gave 60 coins maximum a week, If you do the math: to earn a SINGLE legendary skin it would take 34 weeks of beating ALL weekly challenges to earn. which is downright abysmal and unobtainable for most players.

-OW2 saw for the first time ever locking characters behind the battlepass. in ow1 the spirit of the game was "purchases were cosmetic and heroes wont ever be pay to win" but ow2 said "F that" and began putting new heroes behind the battlepass, in later seasons players could unlock the heroes but new players would have blocked out heroes in their roster they just couldnt play.  that is just so wack considering they would gigabuff the new heroes on release then scale them back(to sell more BP's)

-OW2 introduced new kinds of currency instead of just single gold coins currency, added credits (white) and ultra rare Prisms(purple), this is problematic in general because they capped coin(gold) earnings to 60 max coins a week, but then they also did something very SHIESTY, They used the abundanty earned new credits (white) to buy non-skin items like sprays and emotes etc. !!BUT!! they gave you the option to use your expensive/hard to grind paid currency(gold) to also buy minor items. a small move but ultimately there to trick someone into using their Gold Currency on small items. imagine you spent 9 weeks grinding for gold coins at 60 max a week, only to ACCIDENTALLY buy an 500 credit (white) emote with the wrong currency, they were absolutely counting on that happening. its garbage.

-OW2 decided "people dont know how to team up" (in a team shooter game) so they decided to give EVERY CHARACTER a healing passive that your HP will begin to recover slowly after a few seconds until you next take damage, This is widely considered hand holding and made certain mobile heroes like genji immediatly one sided because they no longer had to rely on healers, rewarding bad solo play styles and further discouraging teamwork. 

-OW2 has a skin Problem, when they release characters and battlepasses they now have a habit of making skins for only the heroes that sell the most skins, while this may seem typical they take it a step further by leaving characters skins out of reach behind battlepasses/skin bundles/Limited events. heroes like Mauga and Venture have no skins to choose from vanilla, while kiriko gets 2 new skins every season because she is the most popular character.

-OW2 developers just simply don't care or have the agency to fix the problems. when criticized time and again by fans for their fumbles, like no more storymode or switch to 6v6, they continued to stick to their guns and say all the changes were good,  and that people are review bombing them undeservedly, while that did happen its a cheap scapegoat for them to dodge their bad decisions and rather than listen or address those issues, they double down. its taken almost 10 seasons for them to come around and most of their "improvements" are just returning old features THEY REMOVED, (i.e Lootboxes, 6v6, no more heroes behind battlepass levels etc.)

-OW2 saw Head developer Papa Jeff Kaplan leaving the project in 2019 and the game has been a soulless husk ever since....

TLDR: Overwatch 1 was such a passion project that was meticously crafted with the gamers in mind, Overwatch 2 and Blizzard saw its success and did everything in their power to Squeeze all they could out of their fans/customers. but the game is still overwatch and lives on regardless. which is a credit to all the love originally put into it. OW2 is a dark shadow of itself, I would not recommend playing, purely because support for this game at this point only supports those who tarnished it.

If you made it this far, thanks for reading, I needed to detail this for posterity sake and I hope overwatch may one day return to its former glory. Lemme know if I missed anything

So, I never played Overwatch 1. When I first started playing ow2, you had to unlock a vast majority of the characters, which kinda sucked. And then, at some point, they made you start with all of those characters, which was a very welcome change. Since then, I've seen the game continue to go mostly upwards, Sure, there's been a few changes that people hated (road rework, sombra rework), but they've been genuinely listening to their players now and reverting things or changing them to a point that people like. They released Stadium, which, while not totally my vibe, is welcome by many others. They also added 6v6 back, which (while I can't comment on how it compares to OW1 6v6), its fun. Fair, I'm not super high ranked, so I don't feel the pain of long queue times (average q time is about 1 or 2 minutes, max for both qp and ranked). I am almost never stuck as a solo tank in 6v6, and the community has been rather nice. I don't get yelled at (er, well, mostly not), and when I do, uh, its fair. No one wants a doomfist that falls off the map. Every minute. 

Moving on, though, the hero design always impresses me. Skins are good, but the actual gameplay feels smooth and I never feel like I reached the skill cap of that particular character, Doomfist always has more I can learn, both in terms of techs but also how to use his movement better. Unlike some other games, where the abilities are super 1 dimensional, this is nice. I can keep learning, instead of just getting better at aiming and being in the right position. 

8/10, good game. Theres room to improve, but its free and fun. I can't really explain. 
...Except for when that DAMN sombra hacks me for the fifth time in one match. Someone kill her. pls.

I said like 1.5 years ago that if they remove the 2 I will give the game a positive review, because it made no sense and was nothing but a reminder of a bad move from the company towards the players. Excluding that and the failed promises that came with it, the game was fun, offered well constructed gameplay and a variety of unique situations on a decently sized map pool. Cosmetics and other microtransactions related things don't matter to me, but compared to other F2P competitors out there they are ok. For an unaffected by the aforementioned things person such as myself (didn't purchase Overwatch, don't care about PvE and other things), I enjoyed my time in the game and with its playerbase. The game's annoyances and gripes most people have with it come from 2 things: the fact that the game's playerbase is really old with a high average playtime, and that when a certain character/character combo is annoying it's usually because they play good compared to the rest of the lobby. I don't see how it lacks behind other mainstream competitive titles, balance-wise or gameplay-wise. The game is just different from most things but not in a bad way, like for example everyone recognizes money in Counter Strike as a resource that you need to track, but can't see ultimates the same for some reason.
When it comes to Marvel Rivals, I don't think the game has a lot to offer in terms of competition, this may sound like cope of course. Regardless of all the details which I will not go into as they do not matter, the presence of Rivals alone has definitely driven Overwatch to be more proactive about things and has given us improvements (some things are just recycled old features) that we might otherwise haven't gotten by now. 
The people who have constant gripes with the game have no actual arguments about the game. They hate it about things like monetization, the company, the company again and its decisions in the past, the overall vibe the game gives off (black W*MAN character ??? = BAD GAME), and a very popular myth about matchmaking being rigged to force you to play longer to achieve the same result (constantly switching between bad teammates vs pro players and good teammates vs bots). Aside from the last one which is not true, if you play bad you will be in a bad rank, none of them have anything to do with the actual game.

When they did remove the 2 from the title I was reluctant to give it a positive review since it had technical issues, but since they fixed them it's ok now and before those issues the game ran and still runs very good, I simply forgot to do it, so it was only a matter of time. Now that the game is nearing its 10th anniversary, it is in a good shape and I definitely think people should try it if they haven't since the "disappointment times" or whatever people refer to the period Overwatch 2 launched as.

Used to love this game during Overwatch 1, bought it 3 times, one for my friend, one for console, and one for the computer. Played it all the time, but stopped after they made Overwatch 2 since I didn't think it would be the same. 

Tried it out recently, and it's almost exactly the same so I fell in love with the game again. Now that I've been playing this game for a couple months, I'm starting to realize that this game is basically getting ruined by all these new characters they're adding. The new characters are making the older characters basically void and useless. It's almost impossible to play Junkrat or Reaper and climb the ranks with how powerful the new characters are. It's not even like a hero shooter anymore, it's more like just a moba. I used to love this game when it was a hero shooter, but it's becoming like every other game with unbalanced heroes, and what's even the point of playing this hero when there's this one? Why play Junkrat and lose when I can just learn a meta hero is what everyone tells me. It used to be a challenge to play Junkrat, now it's just a definite loss whenever I start getting to the higher ranks.

I wish this game would continue being a team game, with the tactical use of different characters and less of a "play this character because they're better than this one." If it was just an opinion, it'd be fine but it's literally true. I'm truly considering trying another hero shooter with how this game is becoming, I was dedicated but now it just feels like they have other ideas of what they want this game to be. Even the Overwatch Championship was showing how certain characters just dominate other ones and there is no point in playing Junkrat, or some other character in competitive to climb because you'll honestly just lose once you get high enough. I also paid for the campaign, but nobody wants to play the campaign and I don't know why, but I'm sad they got rid of the old campaigns in Overwatch 1. They were so good for the storyline, and I liked how you could compete and get on a leaderboard for them.

UPDATE : Recently played Sojourn, and I was wrong. Sojourn is a great character, just wish she had more emotes. Love this game.
