There is an entire world to explore, filled with complex heroes and story-building done, and yet they canceled story missions. After making everyone pay $15 for 3 missions that will probably never get updated or added to.

Now, this is being edited during S1: Conquest, and I do have hope for the future of Overwatch for once! It's exciting that we will have a proper story told through seasons, but that still leaves the Invasion bundle... useless? Plus, one of the fun details of Seasons was them already having their own themes and story missions. Starwatch, for one. Mirrorwatch as well. With themed mythics!

Lately, mythics aren't related to seasons at all, and no longer have fun customization options. As well as heroes who already received Mythics getting another one, when Mercy's was already very cool, especially since it matched with a season. Now, she has two mythics and a mythic weapon? For what? Because she's pretty and it sells well?
There's this habit where only certain heroes receive nice skins and included in bundles, but they're changed massively from who they are supposed to be. Juno constantly being blonde and without her helmet. The same with Kiriko. All of the cutesy skins going to Kiriko when that isn't her character at all genuinely makes me hate her. There's this weird level of favoritism going to one character, and it disgusts me to even play her at times.

With the big updates and events, there's also been a huge lack of mixed competitive game modes. No more competitive deathmatch, or mystery heroes - or even Capture the Flag, which is the base of the Lunar New Years event. But they'd bring back the April Fools event, time and time again? When it was mainly funny ultimate voice lines, and a few alternate abilities? Competitve CTF used to be the face of the Lunar New Years event.
Now, we just receive prop hunt if we're lucky.

Some of the choices genuinely don't make sense. Competitive is the same thing over and over, there should be some duality so that it's fun to continue. Stadium actually has changes that shake things up. Even competitve mystery hero would be amazing, since Mystery Hero is permanent now!

Also, we still want a change for Anran's model, and a change of Pharah's voice actress. How could the developers listen to our voices on a model, but ignore the face of a character?

When games are released they often make the fanbase love or hate the creator. Overwatch was one of these games, a game that brought happiness. A bustling Esports scene, a large, vibrant community, and it seemed like nothing would ever go wrong. Rather, that nothing could go wrong. 

Today, the state of this game is worse than it would be in a poverty stricken slum 

The only feeling overwatch is able to induce upon the people who play it, is the deafening whisper through the keyhole of fate, whispering commands born from pure sadistic malice, without regard for those who care for them.

Developers, what black star hung over the cradle of your birth? have you no shame? with keyboards seemingly fueled by the blood of the lives you've ruined, you stubbornly continue this hellscape of a game for your own corporate greed.

Misanthropy.

Misanthropy must be at an all time high because of this game, not only do other people hate me. But if Every single human came as one, into one entity with the combined emotional weight of all the suffering and death that everyone who has ever been fortunate enough to walk the path of life, it would not equal one nanoangstrom of the pure, unbridled hatred i feel for the people around me.

In response to criticism, they added moira. 

Moira is the incarnation of satan himself, and im telling the complete, and honest truth. 

no aiming, no positioning, no thinking. A character that only requires the mindless keyboard slamming, requiring so little cognitive function that a tartigrade could reach top 500 within a week. Sounds nice right? the majority of humans have an ego that allows them to posses the honor to refuse to play moira. but the one percent of people who do, are obviously completely lacking in human awareness. 

I do not believe these players have sentience, in fact, i deny it outright.

Overall, this game is good if you would like to have both your brain, and all the matter in your body, fueled by pure hate instead of ordinary sustenance.

I'm going to say this now, I am leaving a negative review for Overwatch not on the issues I have with the game but with the issues I have with this port in particular. Overwatch (sans 2) is fun, I have had many good moments in my short time of playing this game buuuuuuut... the Steam Version is so poorly optimised that frankly it seems like Activision Blizzard would much rather you play on Battle.net.

Overwatch on Steam would crash frequently for literally no reason. Benchmarks would simply show the game running as usual, no maximum utilization of CPU, GPU or RAM to be seen. No temperatures outside of what would be considered ordinary. No, this version just crashes and coincidentally the Battle Net version of the game seemingly has no issues... fancy that.

"Well yea, of course it's going to work there! It's Activision Blizzard's property and you SHOULD be playing it there!" Someone will say and to that I strongly disagree. We use Steam cause it keeps our libraries in one easy to find place and allows us to keep minimal software installed on our PCs. Having an entire application for a singular game might be fine for a gacha like Arknights: Endfield and Genshin Impact (though I'd much rather that not be the case whatsoever since I don't see why you want to install an entire bloat launcher when we could just launch the game in Steam) but for a massive release, one that has been ongoing for however long Overwatch 2 has been out?

And even if it wasn't the case, this crashes worse than it's competitors. Marvel Rivals on Epic has issues in Season 0 and 1 I grant you but it's not Season 6 and there has been minimal to no crashes from the game ever since Season 2. I am not against games crashing occassionally because software can do that and code is very fragile BUT when it's crashing every single day, mid-match or menu and you get timed out for a error that Blizzard themselves issue a error code for and recognise is a non-user error... that's just scummy.

If you have to play Overwatch, I recommend console or Battle Net. This release is dog-water in terms of stability and at least Dog Water can argue that it's still drinkable and will quench your thirst. Overwatch can barely say it's a game when it crashes at random and will issue you 12 hour temp bans for issues they cause (yes, I know Rivals did do so in the past but with it's minimal crashes at this point it's a non-issue).

Blizzard, do better. Do seriously better. Do not alienate Steam users with a inferior port on the same platform as your client. Not everyone is playing Starcraft II, Heroes of the Storm, Hearthstone or your awful Warcraft III remaster. Hell, I don't even touch Diablo. I have to have your client to play one game... that's a joke and frankly embarrassing.

Wait a minute, Overwatch [strike]2[/strike] is 10 years old now? Well, I guess what's a better time than any to finally talk about it than on its anniversary as of today?

[u][h2]The beginning[/h2][/u]
Overwatch, a game upon first impression, seems like one that takes a lot of inspiration from Team Fortress, but instantly, it is very apparent this game takes many liberties in being its own product, taking heroic military optimism to a whole new level with lovable characters having quirky humours, colourful attitudes and very expressive animations and voice work that's unlike any other games I've seen before, and I doubt any other hero shooters can ever replicate what makes Overwatch special. 

After first watching its cinematic animated shorts, I fell in love with the game from the start, following it since, finally, a couple of years after its release, I started to play it, and, despite being behind in experience and familiarity compared to other players who had been with the game from the start, I still continue experimenting with different heroes, working with other strangers as a team trying to win games. Enjoying it simply for what it is: a very fun hero shooter. 

[u][h2]What is Overwatch?[/h2][/u]
Anyone new here or anyone who needed a reminder: Overwatch takes place in a highly advanced, futuristic world where there are people with prosthetic limbs; robots who are aware and have souls (you might say); intelligent animals who are capable of communicating with other people and also wield a gun; and magic exists. For how much there is in the game universe, it surprisingly fits the realm of what makes Overwatch feel like... well, Overwatch.

And not only that, Overwatch is a game all about representations, not only in the hero kit design but also in the representation of different people in several countries: their accents, cultural references, languages, and the ways they appear, all done respectfully. There are many other types of character representations, like their sexuality, disability, personality, etc. By now, there are 50 heroes in Overwatch (5 more to be released this year), so even if there isn't a hero (yet?) that doesn't represent your country, you are bound to find one that you can relate to, or you just like their personality or the way they play, even if your playstyle preference is just healbotting, playing body blocker, aiming without aiming, or you just want to go in gun blazing.

[u][h2]The ups and downs[/h2][/u]
It's crazy to think this game lasts this long, first because it's a live service, as well as, over the years, garnishing a lot of bad reputation, which I believe started in the drought year when no major content was released and Blizzard lacked of communication with the player base. Furthermore, the cancellation of the unkept promise of a PvE story, which many fans were anticipating.

Yes, there are certainly a lot of lows in this game's life circles, but there are certainly a lot of highs, like story progression, hero perks, subroles, in-depth hero number abilities stats added, the return of multiple features like loot boxes, healing passives, constant bug fixes, hero balance, hero reworks, and many, many more. And let's not forget all the personal connections with friends or even strangers you play with or against.

One of my memorable moments of this game is when, during quickplay, everyone anonymously decided to stop playing and goof around for a few seconds, emoting and spamming voicelines. It's super rare (thankfully); it probably happened to me 3 times, but it's still a ridiculous experience to remember.

[u][h2]The next steps for the future[/h2][/u]
If there is one thing Blizzard should do, it is add a tutorial for each of the heroes for the new player to use because, as colourful as this game is, it's pretty chaotic and really fast-paced, so it will take you hours to know what each hero does to you because there is a lot by this point. That's why I am a bit hesitant to recommend new players to try this game. Unless you are familiar with Overwatch, and I'll say, why not give it a try? Just know it's a long learning curve to get used to it, but once you do, it's hard to completely remove from your life.

I certainly do; even when I take a break from the game, I later always find a reason to return to it, either to try the new game mode, a new hero, new maps, or heroes I haven't played much or, generally, I just miss Overwatch and want to play it for a bit. I'm not ready to say goodbye to Overwatch yet, and I'm glad they are sticking around for, hopefully, another 10 years, but part of me is scared to know what type of decision that will be made in the future. Will they make 50 more heroes? How long will the story go on for? Will all the Overwatch characters get a proper retirement? Will any more characters get axed off? How long would Aaron Keller be around? 

Part of me is concerned about the future of Overwatch, but a lot of it is optimistic about the future of this beloved franchise. I know there's a lot of hate towards the game these days, and I know I could have easily joined in if I wanted to, but honestly, I don't feel any dislike towards the game. For a lot of wrongs, there's still a lot of good, and I hope that the goods could one day outshine the wrong for a lot of some people in the future. You don't have to agree with my opinion; if you feel a certain way about the game still, that's ok, and it's ok to take a break from the game; there's no need to force yourself to play something that you just don't find any fun in. 

[u][h2]See you in Glory[/h2][/u]
To anyone new here who feels like trying the game out, let me be the first to say [b]WELCOME TO OVERWATCH![/b], and anyone who stuck around, thanks for reading. Let's hope the next 10 years is going to be better than the first.

To everyone, devs, players, fans, newcomers, and lurkers and heroes: have a great day, and high praise to all the teams who worked and continue to support this amazing game.

i play overwatch thru battlenet, but given the recent game updates, i downloaded it on steam to leave what will hopefully be a helpful review for any considering-returning or new players. 

here's the deal: anyone who tells you that overwatch 2 was always good is lying. when it first launched, i was right there alongside everyone else complaining about the removal of 6v6, the pve cancellation, and the absurd monetization model. however, despite quitting the game, i occasionally missed it badly enough that i would log back in and check on how it was doing. then i would see something insane like having to unlock the ability to play new heroes, and immediately quit again. 

when i did one of those check-ins about a year to a year and a half ago, i actually didn't find a reason to quit that time. it was fun again! the insane stuff was removed, and good stuff was steadily being added.

the truth is that overwatch was of a playable quality again about a year/year and a half ago, and it's only gotten better since then. the new dev team repaired the fumbles of the previous one, and then made continued improvements. the game isn't only just now getting good again with this latest flashy update--the devs actually made the very smart move to spend a year silently making the game fun *before* this update, earning back the trust of the community, and *then* made the flashy update to get the attention of people who had previously quit or had never played before. 

if you're considering returning or picking it up, go ahead and give it a try. the changes will probably be overwhelming at first, and you will have to give yourself time to adjust, but the dev team legitimately deserves the hard-earned community trust they managed to get back over the past year or so. i would even go so far as to say that, when you take the nostalgia goggles off and look at objective game quality, this is the most well-designed and polished the game has ever been. this game grew up alongside me, and despite the previous fumbles, it's turned into something i love playing now just as much as i did in 2016.

(also, regarding the controversial monetization issue: bluntly, overwatch needs some form of monetization to stay profitable and pay the team that keeps the game online. as far as free FPS monetization goes, i think it's reasonably unobtrusive. there's no pay-gated gameplay, and that's ultimately what matters. as for cosmetics, there's definitely FOMO-shilling and exorbitantly priced skins, but let's be real. this is an FPS, not a dress-up game. it's pretty easy to let whales finance your gameplay thru their sanrio collab purchases while you just play the game w/ the cosmetics you have. also, lootboxes were added back in, and they're reasonably generous w/ handing them out in events. the free battlepasses also give you some coins if you complete them that you can use to get cosmetics for free every so often.)

(also, yes, 6v6 is back, and has been back for a while now. it has a separate comp and qp queue from 5v5, and there's a fairly dedicated playerbase for it. that said, despite being a passionate 5v5 hater upon its release, i'm quite fond of it now. it's solved some queue time issues and kept ovw feeling modern. give it another try before you dismiss it.)

Right...
Here's for a brutally honest review of what I believe to be one of the worst shooter games out there.
Overwatch. I used to play the first game and I did like the first game quite a bit. It was one of them games I would come back to every so often and play for a bit of fun. I liked it.
I used to main Reaper on Overwatch 1, and I have never played another character. This is just a preference thing but for me there is just too many characters and so I got used to 1 character and that was it.

Overwatch 2 feels like such a waste. They had a perfectly good first game, tried to make a second one. And it flopped... it flopped pretty badly and the amount of bad reviews across Steam and other platforms really speak for themselves. 

First and foremost, they completely replaced Overwatch 1, with Overwatch 2. They shutdown the servers of a perfectly good game and replaced it with this. It's just not the same. Its now free-to-play so they could increase the price of in-game cosmetics to INSANE PRICES. Like what do you mean I can buy a fresh new skin for a character for a measly 20 bucks.
This also lead to them introducing new heroes which are very overpowered but locked behind the new battle pass system which is an absolutely garbage decision for a company to make, making the game pay-to-win from that.
The focus on money is actually predatory.
I have never seen a game change to free-to-play to sell their in-game cosmetics at such high prices and lock overpowered heroes behind a paid battle pass.
They tried to change the game in any way to make it seem like a reasonable choice to create a totally new free game and instead, in my opinion, completely ruined what they had built in Overwatch 1.
I wouldn't be so upset about this if Overwatch had always been like this but because it didn't. It feels so much worse.

Leading up to the release of Overwatch 2, there was so much promise on PvE content which was my main excitement for a new Overwatch. They did not meet this promise and actually fully cancelled it which for me is what made the launch of Overwatch 2 completely useless.
They also removed the 6v6 format and replaced it with 5v5 which was pretty pointless too. It was to make a fast paced environment when 6v6 was perfectly fine and I feel like it only makes it harder for new players. It's so much harder and more chaotic to play.
I mean as of 2026, they have had so much negative feedback about this replacement of 6v6 that they are actively trying to bring it back into the game which I feel its now too far gone.

I also dislike the amount of characters they have added to Overwatch 2. It makes it so much harder to play and get good at the game with the various amount of characters your constantly going up against. Its really confusing especially when there was already so many characters in Overwatch 1, but now there's around 40 in Overwatch 2.

The community also is not great. I mean it never really has but it has only gotten worse since Overwatch 2 was created.

I'll never understand why this game STILL has mixed reviews. The game absolutely deserved to be review bombed during the initial Overwatch 2 release. It was a useless sequel that made everything worse with a lot of broken promises. However in it's current state it's a really solid game, but some people just can't get over the initial launch situation, or have some really dumb complaints.

In the past year they completely threw out many of the old leads responsible for Overwatch 2 being in the state it was in, and made substantial changes that fixed a lot of the games issues. Now they're ditching the whole "2" thing, investing in creating content for the game (they just released 5 new heroes, story content and more) and setting a roadmap that looks really cool. Any complaints about the initial launch of Overwatch 2 are irrelevant now. This isn't even just an Overwatch thing. Almost every Blizzard title has seen massive improvements ever since Epsteins best friend Bobby Kotick and his goons were kicked to the curb.

The game itself is very fun, incredibly well balanced (especially vs it's main competitor, Marvel Rivals) and runs very well for something that looks this nice. The matchmaking system is very good. I can't even rememeber the last game I had where it was a total, one way stomp. Almost every single game is a close one where you actually have to work to get the win and I think that's really nice. I also haven't encountered any cheaters whatsoever since I started playing again which is also very nice. Gameplay wise I can't think of any complaints other than some heroes maybe being a bit too annoying to fight against (Jetpack Cat and the Hamster, mainly).

A lot of the gameplay complaints you see in the negative reviews are usually wrong, or are coming from someone who is bad at the game and doesn't really know what they're talking about (one of the top reviews right now is from someone who has never touched comp, for example)

The microtransactions are a bit annoying, as skins (especially collab skins) can be super expensive. However, they're also entirely optional and don't affect gameplay in any way. On top of that, the battle pass is cheap, gives you premium currency for free and the game itself bombards you with free loot boxes so you'll get a ton of skins just by playing the game. This makes the microtransactions kind of a non-issue, especially since the game is free and they have to fund the continued updates somehow.

Overall, if you haven't played Overwatch in a while or you're a new player, give the game a try. The Steam reviews paint a really bad picture of what the game is actually like. Make your own opinions and you might be surprised with how much you enjoy it

As for the Linux side of things, if you are encountering any stuttering in-game, I would recommend going into your steam settings at downloads and scroll down.

Then ensure these settings are on:

- Enable Shader Pre-caching
- Allow background processing of Vulkan shaders

If the game has to rebuild shaders again after some time playing, look for some launch args to apply globally. You'll find them repeated across ProtonDB and chill out in the practice range with each character until it finishes.

Listen, I’ve been playing Overwatch since the golden days of 2016 and I’m here to tell you this game is still the most beautiful disaster I’ve ever loved. It’s like if a clown car full of colorful superheroes crashed into a competitive ranked hellscape. Every time I boot it up, I tell myself “just one quick game,” and suddenly it’s 4 AM and I’m screaming at my monitor because my team decided to run five DPS and a Symmetra while the enemy has a perfectly coordinated deathball rolling over us like it’s the fall of Rome.
The heroes are absolute legends. I love watching my Genji main friend spam “I need healing” while he’s 300 meters away doing triple backflips off the map. Meanwhile, I’m on support trying to keep these gremlins alive like a single mom at a daycare center for hyperactive murder children. And don’t even get me started on tanks. You pick Winston and suddenly you’re diving in like a brave scientist only to get slept by an Ana who’s been camping in the same corner since the Bronze Age.
Overwatch 2 promised us a brighter future. They said no more 6v6, they said free-to-play, they said new heroes and better experience. What we got was a battle pass that costs more than my monthly groceries and a queue system that makes me wait longer than it takes to finish a college degree. The matchmaking is so good it consistently puts me with players who think “objective” is just a suggestion and “group up” means hiding behind different pieces of cover like it’s a game of hide and seek.
The toxicity? Chef’s kiss. Nothing says “good teamwork” like your own teammate calling you every name in the book because you didn’t perfectly combo your ultimate with theirs. I’ve seen more emotional damage in one comp match than I have in three seasons of reality TV. People throwing, people leaving, people crying about “meta” like their life depends on whether Widowmaker is viable this season.
But here’s the thing that keeps me coming back like a masochist: the gameplay still slaps when it hits. Landing a perfect sleep dart on a charging Reinhardt feels better than therapy. Popping off as Tracer and blinking around like a caffeinated squirrel is pure serotonin. The animations are crisp, the voice lines are iconic, and there’s something magical about yelling “I’m going in!” right before your entire team gets wiped in spectacular fashion.
The monetization is wild though. They’ve got me buying skins for heroes I don’t even play just because they look cool. My wallet has more battle pass levels than my rank has wins. Blizzard really looked at my bank account and said “this man needs more legendary skins for Bastion.”
At the end of the day, Overwatch is like that ex you keep texting. It hurts you, it ghosts you for seasons at a time, it changes completely and acts like nothing happened… but when it’s good? It’s really, really good. I hate it. I love it. I’m uninstalling after this next match.

It's fun, I like it. But I gotta say, it's extremely overwhelming for new-comers (like me). There's like 45 heroes with their own lore and kits as well as an overarching narrative and it's really difficult to keep track of it all.

This follows to the gameplay too. 45 heroes means that there's a combination of... well, a lot of kits. And you never know who can do what unless you play the game for a very long time. Not to mention the UI and VFX overload that is frequent.

If you're fine with that, there's a lot to like here. There's something for everyone's playstyle (I think) and the weapons all feel great. All heroes are free and there are no P2W systems here. The modes are kinda basic, with King of the Hill (Control), Payload (Escort) and Tug of war? (Push) prominantly highlighted. They're not the only ones, though, there's a lot more.

This game is very fun when you put your ego aside and quit telling yourself you're in "elo hell" and realize you are the rank you belong. Its a hero shooter and there always will be the occasional thrower or smurf, but not as often as others make it seem. With that said the game is ok at best when I win and TERRIBLE if I lose.

Well, here it is, my review for Overwatch 2 (OW2). Note that my playtime on Steam isn't accurate, as I play on the Battle . NET version a lot more (my Career Profile states I have 1,854 hours in-game, but that probably doesn't count menus and was probably reset from OW1 to OW2). I have been playing Overwatch since the first game, and before Season 1 of Competitive Play was even a thing, I'm from the old times.

Honestly, this game is what many people have always asked from Triple-A game developers. A Free to Play game where the only monetization is behind cosmetics, and NOT gameplay mechanics. Now, I will give a brief history that this wasn't always the case, and I'm glad that Blizzard / Activision has decided to step back with some of their earlier decisions when OW2 was released.

In this actual Free to Play game, you have a variety of heroes to choose from, Damage heroes, Tank heroes, and Support heroes. Each one of them feel very unique, and as how matches go, think of Team Fortress 2 (TF2) but with MOBA mechanics. You have abilities on cooldown, you got Ultimates, and so on. Unlike MOBAs, you can switch heroes at anytime, at the cost of a % of your Ultimate charge. Recently, they also added "Perks", that you unlock during gameplay per match, and you equip them on-the-fly. It's a great system that makes matches feel more different and more varied.

Netcode feels very good overall, and I don't have any issues with it. In the early days of OW2, and even on OW1, there used to be some disconnects here and there. I think most of the time, things have been a lot more stable. I'm not a Competitive Player, but playing Unranked always felt as it should be. At least where I live, South America, sometimes I can get to North American servers (something that would also happen on OW1, mind you). It seems this rarely happens nowadays, perhaps only when there's barely any people queueing for a specific gamemode on my country.

As for gamemodes, there's a couple to choose from. You have your standard game of Overwatch, where you can play Unranked, Competitive, or Versus CPU bots. Competitive also include a Role Queue and Open Queue modes. Role Queue is 5v5, with 2 Healers, 2 Damage Heroes and 1 Tank, while Open Queue lets anyone choose any role during the match, but tanks are limited to only 2 (you can have a match with 4 Healers and 2 Tanks, or 4 Damage heroes and 2 Tanks, etc etc.).

There's also Arcade, which are more of the "party-game" game modes that Overwatch brings. Sometimes you get some that are Event-exclusive, that changes each Hero moveset, or a completely different gamemode altogether (like Prophunt). I haven't mentioned it yet, but in a match, no one can pick the same hero more than once, from your team, but on Arcade, there's a mode for No Limits that lets you pick as many times the same hero. My favorite Arcade gamemode has to be Total Mayhem. It's basically what if Overwatch was Mario Kart, and it's a lot of fun. There's actual strategy behind this frenetic gamemode, and there's a couple Heroes I only play there. Lastly, sometimes there's "Classic" mode, where you can play as how OW1 was years ago. Honestly... the game was really jank before, you can really see the difference if you play that gamemode and what is currently on OW2.

Finally, there's Stadium, which is a pretty recent addition to Overwatch. It makes the MOBA elements on Overwatch even more apparent (no Hero switching, purchasing upgrades at the start of a match, and so on). I have played a few matches and it's a lot of fun, you can really feel the Hero you picked becoming stronger, and there's a lot of fun combinations you can go with. You have stat upgrades, as well more "Major" upgrades that changes your abilities more. Stat upgrades can be purchased at anytime and you can hold 6 at once, but Major upgrades can be purchased every few rounds (with a specific round set, depending if it's Unranked Stadium or Competitive). Though I wish I could disable the Announcer, he is a little too over-energetic.

I think I will now talk about Monetization and Cosmetics. So, as mentioned, this game is truly Free to Play (like 99%). Cosmetics are only for your customization needs, and, does not affect gameplay in any shape or form. With that said, cosmetics can be indeed quite "pushy", and whether you like that or not, it really goes from person to person. I suppose it's the "price" of Free to Play. This game have a lot of Collabs with other IPs. We have had Porsche, My Hero Academia, and even Persona. The skins are very well made, but I do believe some of them might hurt Hero Recognition (Junker Queen's Mob Boss skin is a great example). Most Heroes have very clear animation stances, posture, and particle effects, but still.

Even more surprisingly, is that the game includes Loot Boxes, but not paid ones. You can get Loot Boxes by either playing the game with Weekly Challenges, or get a couple from the Battle Pass. Yes, there is a Battle Pass, and for the Free tier, you can get a couple Loot Boxes, but there's a few more on the Premium tier. Loot Boxes can unlock items that was previously on the shop (!) as well the Battle Pass. I'm not sure if there's items that are unable to be obtained from Loot Boxes, but I have unlocked quite a few things just by playing.

Now, as any Free to Play game, this game is going to make you stick as long as possible playing. You get Daily and Weekly challenges, you get Event challenges, Hero challenges, Lifetime challenges, and so on. Each Hero has their own Progression system that, if you are one of those people who like to try 100% everything what is available for Free, well, goodluck, because it's a very long road ahead. 

I think one critic I have for the game is regards to most of the Support heroes. I feel like there's no good indicator of how much you've healed someone. Yes, there are sound effects, but I like to play while listening to music. Outside Mercy, Zenyatta and Wuyang, there's no real Heal Indicator than Health Bars on top of each one of your allies. Playing as Moira feel like I'm wasting my Heal resources without knowing for sure if an ally is fully healed. Spectator shows healthbars of both allies and enemies on top of the screen, and I feel like this could be added in-game, but only for allies. I also wish there was a small indicator if an ally's Ultimate ability is ready, as opposed in having to open the Scoreboard to check. Basically how it works with Sombra when she hacks enemies, and shows the Ultimate icon, but at all times on allies.

There's also another small critic in regards to performance once you launch the game. For some reason, the game struggles a lot on the first few minutes, so I always recommend going to Practice Range before heading to any match. Once you give it sometime, the game will run properly without any freezes or framedrops. This could be because I play windowed, but I have read online other people having the same problem. When you close the game, it can also take quite a while for the process to end completely, for some reason.

The only "Paid" gameplay thing is the PvE missions, which I have not played. When those missions were added to OW2, during the same day that it came to Steam, a lot of people thought PvE was gone for good, but it wasn't the case. What was removed was the Talent Tree system, where you could change the way your Hero played on PvE missions. Since then, however, it seems that we may never get any other PvE mission due to changes on the structure of the development (Microsoft acquisition) and layoffs. So I would just ignore those PvE missions, and Blizzard doesn't even try to push them anyway. It's on a very small corner on the Shop, and well, if it's fun or not, I dunno, but I heard it's kinda average and the inclusion of the Talent Tree wouldn't bring much.

All in all, I recommend this game. It has improved a lot since it was originally released: 9/10.

You will never get the 1st year of OW1's experience ever again.
If you bought OW1 back then, congrats, you've been scammed like me. 
I only tried OW2 during S17 after getting harassed by a friend for more than a month about it. So that's few years of hiatus. Honestly for OW2, S17 was in a pretty good state despite all the obvious flaws. Not perfect, still unbalanced af and devs won't do sh*t about it for months (Sombra with a ban rate being insanely high, Sojourn and Freja being absurd DPS, which makes others DPS heroes look bad etc.). At least with all the sh*t I've seen from afar before touching that forbidden crap again, it was probably the best state of OW2. Stadium managed to make the game fun once again despite the lack of characters and balance, and 6v6 is getting somewhat close to what OW1 used to be, without its terrible metas it used to bring like GOAT or triple barrier. 

Then S18 just dropped, they just decided to make another extremely dumb decision by copying LoL even more without asking themselves the most basic question "Why is draft working on LoL?".
And then they decided to add Draft AND a [b]no-mirror clause[/b] to stadium in a very limited roster where most people can play maybe 2 or 3 characters max.
If you don't get what's going on, they basically added the same kind of draft you get in your fav moba, but you can also not pick the same character as one of your opponents. It would've been okay if Overwatch had a very complete roster, except Overwatch's roster is already lacking compared to generic mobas AND it's even more limited because not all characters have been updated for Stadium, which makes it even more limited. [b]There's a total of 24 characters in stadium at the time I'm writing this (S18). [/b]
At least watching the mercy mains melting is somewhat fun, but this sh*t cannot work because there's just not enough characters in Stadium. And a whole team managed to greenlight that. But it's Blizzard so at this point I'm not surprised anymore by their ability to shoot themselves in the foot. They basically made a fun mode that was managing to get some people back in the game, then killed it any% by just being dumber than a heir of an incestuous family over 3 generations.  
Update : It was so bad (on the top of being buggy) they reverted the no-mirror clause days later because the guy in charge of stadium couldn't run away anymore after disabling social medias just because everyone told him this was a terrible idea when they announced it.

Budget cut is also very obvious, for some time they put a bugged 3D mercy model as the menu background where her hair clipped through her arms. Nobody bothered to check beforehand, nobody bothered to fix it either. This was for advertising OWL with a 40 dollars skin. It's getting lazier. They just care about releasing every 3 weeks skins for the same egirls who still spend an unholy amount of money in this game (understand "Mercy" and "Kiriko" mains).  
Skins are obviously overpriced, PVE is dead on arrival because they only cared about updating the store in the first place. From what I've read on the net, appparently all previous PVE content in OW1 (archives) got killed because of the engine's rework? How? OW2 was advertised for PVE but you killed the already existing PVE, only to make a half-assed paid and overpriced PVE later? Just directly call me a bunch of slurs at this point, it'll be faster.
Update: Also they cut budget so hard they got rid of any QoL  and probably encouraged vibe coding because S18 was probably the update with the biggest amount of bugs I've ever seen in this game since 2016. 1 hero had to be disabled for a while, 2 others were so badly bugged they could ruin the game (McCree/Cass/Whatever you call him now 3 shotting a tank should speak for itself) but somehow they didn't deserve to be disabled aswell lol. They keep pushing hotfixes and making terrible balance decisions no one asked for (like nerfing Hog again for literally no reason, dropping him to a sub 40% winrate, while still taking their time for Sombra who has a 90% ban rate). The draft in stadium is also bugged enough to allow exploits where you pick the same character, you can end up fighting 2 pharamercy at once basically. 
Oh and remember the simple "leave as group" checkbox when leaving a game as soon as it's over? They broke it somehow. No matter what you do now, you will leave without your group, and they don't seem to be able to fix? What the hell? 
And that's just few of them in the long list of bugs they introduced. 
If I started listing all bugs of this season, this review would be just too long (it already is tbh).

Support role queues are still super long compared to DPS and Tank because most of the population remaining are main support, and sadly OW is the only shooter which brings a somewhat satisfying experience for them. Everyone else is able to move to others shooters, and nobody can blame them for that.

Lucio ball arrived in September (lol) but they forced some horrible 3rd person view and you can't switch back to 1st person because "F*ck you". 

Also, it's happening more and more often, we're getting queued in the wrong servers. You can get a whole lobby of EUW being placed in Asia servers with +250ms. It happens in EVERY mode. Small indie company, please understand. This was already a thing months ago but it just seems to get increasingly worse over time. OH and [b]now you can't make a custom lobby if you're not endorsed lvl 4. This is their solution for gatekeeping custom games and saving server costs. I'm not joking. [/b]

[h1]⚠ Negative review — solely due to server instability. Will change if fixed.[/h1]

[i]I genuinely enjoy Overwatch 2. The gameplay, heroes, and design are all solid. This review is not about any of that. It is marked negative for one specific, technical reason: [b]persistent and unfixable lag spikes on the South America (GBR1) server[/b] — a problem that, after extensive research and testing, I can confirm is entirely on Blizzard's side.[/i]

[hr][/hr]

[h2]The Problem[/h2]

Every match, without exception, I experience [b]3 to 4 random lag spikes[/b] lasting a few seconds each. Ping jumps from a stable ~25–50ms to 150–300ms+ and then returns to normal. The game becomes unplayable for those moments — rubberbanding, missed abilities, enemies teleporting. It happens at any time of day, including late at night when servers are least loaded.

[hr][/hr]

[h2]What I Ruled Out (Everything On My End)[/h2]

Before blaming Blizzard, I spent a significant amount of time systematically eliminating every possible client-side cause:

[b]Wi-Fi adapter (MediaTek MT7921):[/b]
[list]
[*]Disabled U-APSD Support, set Roaming Aggressiveness to Lowest, enabled Maximum Performance power mode
[*]Locked adapter to 5GHz / 802.11ax mode
[*]Disabled Large Send Offload, Packet Coalescing, ARP/GTK WoWLAN offloads
[*]Installed the Lenovo-packaged driver (v3.0.1.1325), confirmed as the community's recommended fix for MT7921 latency issues
[*]Enabled MSI Mode (Message Signaled Interrupts) via MSI Utility v3 to reduce DPC latency
[*]Disabled WLAN AutoConfig background scanning via CMD
[/list]

[b]Windows / network stack:[/b]
[list]
[*]Disabled TCP Auto-Tuning
[*]Flushed DNS, switched to Cloudflare (1.1.1.1)
[*]Set Overwatch 2 process priority to High in Task Manager
[*]Closed all background apps, cloud sync, Steam overlay, Discord overlay
[*]Opened required Blizzard ports on the router
[/list]

[b]Routing:[/b]
[list]
[*]Used [b]NoPing[/b] (a Brazilian gaming VPN/optimizer specifically designed for Blizzard routing) — spikes persisted identically
[*]Confirmed via [b]Ctrl+Shift+N netgraph[/b] in-game that I am correctly placed on [b]GBR1[/b] (the São Paulo SA server), not being misrouted to NA
[*]Tested at multiple times of day including late at night — no difference
[/list]

[b]Parallel diagnostics:[/b]
[list]
[*]Ran [code]ping -t 8.8.8.8[/code] alongside gameplay — Google ping stayed perfectly stable during OW2 spikes, confirming the issue is [b]not local[/b]
[*]Monitored netgraph: [b]LAT itself spikes[/b] (not just IND/jitter), which means the problem originates server-side
[*]All other games (including other competitive shooters) run without any issues on the same connection and hardware
[/list]

[hr][/hr]

[h2]What The Community Confirmed[/h2]

This is not an isolated issue. After researching Blizzard's own forums, Steam discussions, and Reddit:

[list]
[*]GBR1 server instability has been [b]documented in recurring waves since at least October 2023[/b], with major outbreaks in Feb 2024, Sep 2024, May 2025 (Season 16 midseason patch), and Oct–Nov 2025 (post-Stadium update)
[*]Dozens of threads with identical symptoms exist on the official Blizzard forums — players across Brazil, Argentina, Chile, and Peru on different ISPs all report the same spikes simultaneously
[*]Blizzard issued [b]exactly one blue post[/b] in response to all of this (a standard troubleshooting request in Oct 2023), with no visible follow-up and no patch notes ever referencing SA server fixes
[*]One thread from November 2025 stated the problem has existed [b]since 2018[/b]
[/list]

[hr][/hr]

[h2]Conclusion[/h2]

After exhausting every client-side fix known to the community — adapter drivers, MSI interrupts, routing optimizers, network stack tweaks, and diagnostic tools — the verdict is clear:

[quote=Blizzard Forum, Nov 2025]The SA servers have had latency issues since 2018. We just want some confirmation that the devs acknowledge what is going on.[/quote]

[b]The lag spikes are caused by instability in Blizzard's GBR1 infrastructure[/b], not by anything the player can fix. South American players deserve the same server reliability that other regions take for granted.

[i]I will update this review to positive the moment Blizzard demonstrably stabilizes the GBR1 servers. The game itself is good. The infrastructure is not.[/i]
